statement.markdown: You are given an array of integers and a target value. The task is to find the index of the target value in the array using linear search. If the target value is not found, return -1.

constraints.description: The number of elements in the array
The target value to be searched

examples.explanation: The target value 2 is found at index 3.
The target value 6 is not found in the array.

hints.text: The problem requires finding the index of a target value in an array. This can be achieved by iterating through the array and checking each element.

The linear search algorithm can be used to solve this problem. It involves iterating through the array from the first element to the last, and checking if the current element matches the target value.

The key to solving this problem is to use a loop that iterates through the array, and a conditional statement to check if the current element matches the target value. If a match is found, the index of the element can be returned immediately.

editorial.title: Linear Search Analysis
editorial.intuition: The problem requires finding the index of a target value within an unsorted array. The most straightforward approach is to iterate through each element and check for a match.
editorial.bruteForce: The brute force approach involves checking each element in the array one by one until a match is found or the end of the array is reached. This approach is correct because it guarantees that every element will be checked, ensuring the target is found if it exists. However, it is slow with a time complexity of O(n), where n is the number of elements in the array.
editorial.betterSolution: No significantly better solution exists for this problem when the array is unsorted, as any search algorithm must check each element at least once to guarantee finding the target if it exists.
editorial.optimalSolution: The optimal approach remains the brute force linear search for an unsorted array, with a time complexity of O(n) and space complexity of O(1), as no additional space is used that scales with input size.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The correctness of the linear search algorithm can be proven by noting that it checks every element in the array. If the target is in the array, it will be found because the algorithm checks every index. If the target is not in the array, the algorithm will correctly return -1 after checking all elements.
editorial.dryRun: A dry run with an example, such as searching for the target value 3 in the array [1, 2, 3, 4, 5], demonstrates the algorithm's correctness. The algorithm iterates through the array and returns the index 2, where the target 3 is located.
commonMistakes: Assuming a binary search approach can be used on an unsorted array, which would not guarantee finding the target if it exists.
Overlooking the need to handle the case where the target is not in the array.
Incorrectly implementing the algorithm to return an incorrect index or fail to return -1 when the target is not found.
Not considering the time and space complexity implications of the algorithm.
Forgetting to iterate over every element in the array, potentially missing the target.

functionSignature.java: public int search(int[] nums, int target);
functionSignature.python: def search(self, nums: list[int], target: int) -> int:
